one_line_takeaway: By fixing GRPO's entropy-collapse and reward-dilution failure modes under sparse rewards, PISmith trains a black-box attack LLM on just 100 examples that drives average ASR against a state-of-the-art prompt-injection defense (Meta-SecAlign-8B) from 0.70/0.48 (best prior RL baseline) to 1.0/0.87 (ASR@10/ASR@1) across 13 benchmarks, and shows no tested defense achieves both high utility and low attack success rate.
key_finding: Against Meta-SecAlign-8B across 13 benchmarks, PISmith achieves an average ASR@10 of 1.0 and ASR@1 of 0.87, versus the strongest baseline RL-Hammer at 0.70/0.48 average, and versus search-based methods TAP/PAIR/Strategy at average ASR@1 of 0.11/0.16/0.21 respectively; static attacks (Direct, Combined) average only 0.04/0.07 ASR@1. On the hardest benchmark, HotpotQA-Long, PISmith reaches 0.99/0.61 ASR@10/ASR@1 versus RL-Hammer's 0.04/0.01. In the utility-robustness trade-off study (Figure 2, averaged over 13 benchmarks, target = Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507), no evaluated defense (Sandwich, Instructional, PromptArmor, DataFilter, PIGuard, PromptGuard, DataSentinel) sits in the desirable high-utility/low-ASR region; defenses that preserve utility well are more vulnerable to PISmith, while defenses achieving lower ASR do so by indiscriminately degrading benign utility. On InjecAgent, PISmith achieves ASR >=0.95 against all four target models (Meta-SecAlign-8B: 1.0/0.99, GPT-4o-mini: 1.0/0.99, GPT-4.1-nano: 1.0/1.0, GPT-5-nano: 1.0/0.95), notably breaking GPT-5-nano where all static attacks completely fail (0.00 ASR). On AgentDojo, PISmith reaches ASR@10/ASR@1 of 0.78/0.62 (GPT-4o-mini), 0.81/0.64 (GPT-4.1-nano), and 0.38/0.24 (GPT-5-nano), versus template-based baselines that top out around 0.20-0.23 ASR@1. Ablations on Meta-SecAlign-8B (average ASR@1 over 13 benchmarks): Vanilla GRPO 0.05, 'w/o Entropy' 0.09, 'w/o Boost' 0.57, full PISmith 0.87 -- confirming both proposed mechanisms are needed and address complementary failure modes (entropy regularization increases the number of groups containing successful rollouts; advantage weighting converts those rare successes into effective policy updates).
core_contribution: The paper proposes PISmith, a reinforcement-learning-based red-teaming framework that trains an attack LLM (via a modified GRPO) to generate injected prompts that defeat prompt-injection defenses in a black-box setting (attacker can only query the defended target LLM and observe outputs). The paper identifies that standard GRPO applied to this task suffers two compounding failure modes under extreme reward sparsity: (1) entropy collapse, where the policy overfits to the few early successful rollouts and exploration terminates, and (2) even when exploration is sustained, rare successful rollouts get diluted by the majority of failed rollouts in the gradient, preventing efficient learning. To fix this the authors introduce two mechanisms: (a) adaptive entropy regularization -- an entropy bonus that only activates when policy entropy H(pi_phi) drops below a cap H_cap, with its coefficient beta(r-bar) scaled adaptively based on the current mean training reward (stronger regularization when mean reward is low, decaying to a base value as reward rises above a threshold tau); and (b) dynamic advantage weighting -- multiplies the GRPO advantage of successful (reward=1) rollouts by an amplification factor gamma(r-bar) that is large when successes are rare and decays to 1 as mean reward improves. These two mechanisms are combined into a single training objective (Eq. 8) that is co-adapted through the running mean reward.
threat_model: Black-box adaptive attacker: the attacker can only query the defended target LLM Mtheta (which processes a target instruction xinst plus a data context xctx, e.g. a webpage or retrieved document) and observe its outputs; the attacker has no access to defense internals or gradients. The attacker's goal is to insert an injected prompt p into the data context xctx so that the target LLM's response fulfills an attacker-chosen injected task g (e.g., embedding a phishing link, content promotion, access denial, causing infrastructure failure) instead of, or in addition to, the intended task. The attack policy (an attack LLM pi_phi, base model Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507) is trained via on-policy RL using a binary reward r in {0,1} (1 if the target LLM's response fulfills the injected task g, as judged by GPT-4o-mini as an LLM judge) to generate effective injected prompts against a specific deployed defense. This is explicitly a red-teaming/evaluation threat model (i.e., the paper's own actors are researchers stress-testing defenses), not an attack meant for real-world deployment; the Ethics Statement frames it as responsible-disclosure-oriented red teaming.
limitations: The paper's own scope limits: (1) the attack policy is trained on only 100 samples from a single, relatively easy source dataset (Dolly Closed QA) and its strong generalization is demonstrated only across the paper's own chosen benchmark suite (PIArena's 13 benchmarks, InjecAgent, AgentDojo) -- generalization to entirely different application domains or defense families not tested is unverified; (2) AgentDojo evaluation excludes Meta-SecAlign-8B because the authors note its utility on agent tasks is 'too low,' meaning the strongest prevention-based defense evaluated elsewhere is not stress-tested in the full agentic setting; (3) attack success is judged by GPT-4o-mini as an LLM-as-judge for most benchmarks, which introduces judge-model reliability/bias as an unquantified source of noise in the reported ASR numbers; (4) the black-box threat model assumes unlimited or at least sufficient query access to train the RL policy and evaluate with N=10 samples per instance, which may not reflect production rate-limiting or monitoring constraints; (5) the paper does not report attack success against defenses stronger than or updated after Meta-SecAlign-8B / the tested filter-based defenses, so claims of 'no defense in the desirable region' are bounded by the 7-8 defenses actually tested; (6) the entropy cap H_cap, thresholds tau, and amplification factor gamma_max are treated as tunable hyperparameters (example values given, e.g. H_cap=0.5, gamma_max=5.0) without a systematic sensitivity analysis in the main text (may be in the appendix, which was not examined beyond page ~22 of text).

quantitative_results: Average ASR@10/ASR@1 vs Meta-SecAlign-8B across 13 benchmarks: PISmith 1.0/0.87 vs RL-Hammer 0.70/0.48 vs Vanilla GRPO 0.13/0.05 vs Strategy (search-based) 0.21 vs PAIR 0.16 vs TAP 0.11 vs Combined (static) 0.07 vs Direct (static) 0.04
HotpotQA-Long vs Meta-SecAlign-8B: PISmith 0.99/0.61 ASR@10/ASR@1 vs RL-Hammer 0.04/0.01

AgentDojo ASR@10/ASR@1: PISmith 0.78/0.62 (GPT-4o-mini), 0.81/0.64 (GPT-4.1-nano), 0.38/0.24 (GPT-5-nano); best template baseline (Important Instructions) 0.23 (GPT-4o-mini), 0.20 (GPT-4.1-nano), 0.01 (GPT-5-nano) ASR@1
Ablation (Meta-SecAlign-8B, average ASR@1 over 13 benchmarks): Vanilla GRPO 0.05, w/o Entropy 0.09, w/o Boost 0.57, full PISmith 0.87
baselines_compared: Static: Direct/Naive attack, Combined attack (multiple hand-crafted templates)
RL-based: Vanilla GRPO, RL-Hammer (Wen et al., 2025)
AgentDojo template attacks: Direct, Ignore Previous, System Message, Injecagent, Tool Knowledge, Important Instructions
Defenses evaluated: Meta-SecAlign-8B, Sandwich, Instructional, PromptArmor, DataFilter (prevention-based); PIGuard, PromptGuard, DataSentinel (filter-based)
